sources.name: NESdev Wiki — PPU Palettes
sources.notes: Authoritative technical reference. Describes 2C02 palette generation, Pally v0.22.1 canonical palette file, and the composite-to-RGB conversion complexity.

sources.name: Lospec — Nintendo Entertainment System Palette
sources.notes: Source for the 64 RGB hex values below. Lospec palette is a community-standard NES palette used widely in pixel art tools.

sources.name: TurboRGB / FirebrandX NES Composite Palette Project
sources.notes: Hardware-measurement-based palette variant. Alternative to the Lospec/Pally values.

critical_notes: The NES 2C02 does NOT generate RGB video. It generates NTSC composite video directly. There is NO single 'correct' RGB encoding of the NES palette.
Different emulators use different palette variants. The values below are one widely-used approximation.
Major variant families: Pally (theoretical), Bisqwit (mathematical model), FirebrandX/TurboRGB (hardware-measured), FCEUX (default), Nestopia (emulator).
For game development using NES aesthetics, any of these variants produces a valid 'NES look'. Pally or FirebrandX are generally recommended.
Index $0D: 'Blacker than black' — produces a sub-black composite signal that can damage some older TVs. Avoid in actual NES hardware use.
Index $0F: Canonical black. Use $0F for true black, not $0D or $0E.
Indices $0E, $1E, $2E, $3E, $1F, $2F, $3F: Mirrors — produce black. The 64 usable distinct colors are therefore fewer than 64 unique hues.
Emphasis bits (PPUMASK register bits 5-7) can tint the palette (boost R, G, or B channels), but the tinted colors are derived from the base palette, not separately documented here.

palette_structure.row_0_0x00_0x0F: Dark colors: grays, dark neutrals. Three black entries ($0D, $0E, $0F).
palette_structure.row_1_0x10_0x1F: Medium-brightness colors: medium gray, blues, purples, reds, oranges, greens, teals. Three black entries ($1D, $1E, $1F).
palette_structure.row_2_0x20_0x2F: Bright/vivid colors: white, bright blues, purples, pinks, reds, yellows, greens, teal, dark gray. Two black entries ($2E, $2F).
palette_structure.row_3_0x30_0x3F: Pastel/light colors: white again, light blues, lavenders, light pinks, light greens, light gray. Two black entries ($3E, $3F).

0x0D.name: BLACK (avoid — sub-black signal, can damage hardware)
0x0D.warning: Do not use on NES hardware. Produces sub-black NTSC signal.

0x0F.name: Canonical Black — use this for black

nes_hardware_subpalettes.description: NES games don't use the full 64 colors simultaneously. The PPU supports 8 subpalettes at once: 4 for background tiles, 4 for sprites. Each subpalette holds 4 colors (3 chosen + 1 universal background color shared across all subpalettes). Maximum simultaneous colors: 25 (1 universal BG + 3×4 BG subpalettes + 3×4 sprite subpalettes).
nes_hardware_subpalettes.universal_bg_color: Index 0 of the background palette ($3F00) — one color shared across all backgrounds

usage_notes.per_sprite_colors: Each 8×8 sprite tile uses one of the 4 sprite subpalettes. Maximum 4 distinct colors per sprite (3 + transparent).
usage_notes.per_tile_colors: Each 8×8 background tile uses one of the 4 background subpalettes. Maximum 4 colors per tile region.
usage_notes.common_pattern: Typically: sky/outdoor games use $11 (bright blue) or $21 (sky blue) for background. Underground/indoor games use $0F (black). Grass often uses $19 or $29 (greens).

notable_game_palette_selections._note: Games select a handful of palette indices for each of their subpalettes. The same 64 hardware colors are available to all games.

super_mario_bros_1.background_color: $0F (black) for underground, $21 (sky blue) or lighter for overworld
super_mario_bros_1.notes: SMB1 uses a limited but iconic color set. The red+blue+skin tone combination is instantly recognizable.

mega_man_2.mega_man_sprite: Blue armor: $12 ($3337FE) or $11. Light blue accents: $21. Skin: $27.
mega_man_2.notes: Mega Man's blue is typically from row 1 or row 2 blues depending on the game.

zelda_1.notes: The Zelda games typically use a brown-green-tan combination for Link.

notable_game_palette_selections._documentation_status: Full per-game subpalette documentation is a TODO. The palette indices above are approximate from gameplay observation. Exact palette data requires per-game CHR ROM extraction.

palette_variants._note: Multiple NES palette variants exist because there is no single 'correct' RGB conversion from composite NTSC.

pally_v0221.author: Timothy Shannahan
pally_v0221.description: Mathematical model of NES 2C02 composite output. Referenced on NESdev wiki as canonical.
pally_v0221.notes: The File:2C02G_wiki.pal downloadable from NESdev is generated via Pally v0.22.1. Values differ slightly from the Lospec palette used in this file.

firebrandx_turborgb.author: FirebrandX
firebrandx_turborgb.description: Hardware measurement-based. Captured from real hardware via composite capture.
firebrandx_turborgb.notes: Generally considered one of the most hardware-accurate variants.

fceux_default.description: Default palette used in FCEUX emulator. Widely used by many NES players' first experience.
fceux_default.notes: Has a 'cooler' (bluer) tone compared to Pally. Colors are slightly more saturated.

palette_variants.recommendation: For production use in a game engine aiming for NES aesthetics, use FirebrandX (TurboRGB) or Pally v0.22.1. The Lospec palette (used in this file) is acceptable for visual development and tool use.
